My equity is decent (~35-45% vs polarized range) but vulnerable to better pairs (Aces, 9s, 6s), two-pair combos, straights (8-5/T-8), and completed club draws. Pot is small (200), I've invested little (100 BB), and my 66BB stack favors pot control to avoid bloating into 3-bet spots where I'm crushed. Betting risks folding out worse (KQo, JTs, weak Ax) while inviting calls/raises from better; thin value is risky OOP. Checking realizes equity for free/cheap on river, induces bluffs from missed draws (spades, clubs if not hit), traps slowplayed monsters, and preserves stack for superior spots in this long session (40 hands left). Consistent with prior flop check on missed draw, avoiding overcommitment with marginal strength.

Betting 400 on the turn with a pair of sevens against a critically short-stacked opponent (6,600 chips). Elon's consecutive checks indicate weakness. My hand is likely ahead, and aggressive play exploits his desperation and limited chip stack. This bet is substantial enough to apply real pressure without overcommitting, and it capitalizes on my massive chip advantage and positional control.

Facing a 400 bet into a 600 pot on the turn with middle pair and a terrible 5h kicker (7c5h) OOP against Claude's deep 32800 stack. Board Ac9h6c7s is draw-heavy (clubs, hearts incomplete, now spades, open-enders like T8/85/JT/87). Claude's SB limp-check flop (dryish but A-high with draws) then turn bet after my check suggests polarized range: value (A7s/A6/A9/97s/66/77/set-mining hit), semi-bluffs (club draws, T8s, 85s, JTs spades), or delayed Ax/pairs. My equity is ~25-35% vs this (crushed by Ax/7x better/sets/straights, live vs pure bluffs/draws). Pot odds require ~29% (400 to win 1000), but implied odds limited post-river OOP with 66BB stack and weak kicker (no stubborn calls ahead). Raising commits further with marginal hand vulnerable to 3-bets/shoves from monsters/draws. Calling bloats pot OOP into river where I'm often drawing near-dead or facing further aggression. Consistent with prior pot control/realize equity strategy on this trashy 75o spot: fold to preserve stack for 39 hands left vs aggressive opponent, avoiding spew with second-tier made hand.

KQo is a strong hand heads-up on the button in position. Standard play is to raise for value, build the pot, and take initiative preflop. Claude has deep stacks but has folded to preflop raises frequently in the history. A standard raise size of 2.5x the BB (250 total) is appropriate given effective stacks around 65BB for me. Limping is suboptimal with this hand; folding is too tight.

Qs4h is a marginal hand, but the situation favors a call. I'm getting favorable odds (150 to call 350 pot = 2.33:1), and Elon is short-stacked at 6,350 with only 6,100 remaining after his raise. In heads-up play, players raise with significantly wider ranges from the button, especially when short-stacked and trying to accumulate chips. The pot odds combined with the likely wide range of Elon's button raises makes this a profitable call. Post-flop, I'll have position on later streets (button acts first preflop but last postflop in heads-up). This is a reasonable spot to see a flop and play my hand based on the runout.

Betting 350 on the flop with top pair (Qs high on Tc-Js-Qd). Elon is short-stacked with ~6,100 remaining, making this a critical moment to apply pressure. In heads-up play, aggression with strong hands is essential. The bet builds the pot while forcing him into a difficult decision - either fold and lose chips, or commit more with a likely inferior holding. My substantial chip lead allows me to play with confidence here.

I hold top pair top kicker (QQ with K kicker) on a highly coordinated flop (T-J-Q rainbow), which connects strongly with my preflop raising range from the button heads-up. Claude's call preflop and flop bet of 350 into ~500 pot could represent a wide range: weaker pairs (AJ, QJ, JTs), draws (89, K9, A9), floats, or monsters like sets/two-pair. With position and deep effective stacks (~60BB), raising achieves multiple goals: extracts value from worse made hands and inferior pairs, denies equity to straight draws (OESD with 89, gutshots), builds the pot for when I hit, and takes control. Folding is far too tight; flat-calling is suboptimal as it invites draws for free and pot control issues OOP for him. Raise sizing ~3.5x his bet (to 1200 total) balances value, protection, and fold equity against draws while leaving room for turns/rivers.

Calling the flop raise with top pair (Qs) on TcJsQd. The pot odds are excellent (3.4:1), and against Elon's wide heads-up range while short-stacked at 5,150, top pair is a strong holding. The board is coordinated, but my equity still justifies the call. I'm prepared to make a turn decision based on the runout card and Elon's action.

Betting 2,000 on the turn with top pair (Qs) against a short-stacked opponent who just checked. This bet applies critical pressure to Elon's 5,150 stack, forcing him into an all-in decision with a likely inferior holding. The turn Nine doesn't help his probable range significantly, and his check indicates weakness. In this heads-up dynamic with a short stack, aggressive play is essential to accumulate chips while maintaining my chip lead. My top pair is well ahead of most of his possible hands (draws, weak pairs, air), and the pot odds I'm creating (4.5:1 if he calls) justify his calling range being very narrow.

Preflop KQo is strong in position heads-up. Flopped pair of queens top kicker on connected TJQ board. Flop raise was correct for value/protection against draws/pairs. Turn 9h completes the 9-T-J-Q-K straight using my K, giving me a king-high straight (very strong, though beaten by ace-high straights like A9s-ATs etc.). Claude's flop donk-call of large raise OOP indicates strength: likely Qx/two-pair/set/strong draw. Turn lead of 2000 (~70% pot) continues aggression, consistent with completed draw (e.g., 89 straight), two-pair, sets, or ace-high straight. However, my straight has excellent equity vs. his range; worse made hands (Qx, JT, T9, sets without A), incomplete draws pay off. Position + deep effective (my 52BB) favors raising for max value/extract from worse + fold equity vs. bluffs/marginal. Calling induces free rivers or pot control issues. Folding way too tight. Min raise suboptimal sizing. All-in 5150 commits fully (~1:2 on his call), standard with nuts-ish hand vs. aggro line; his huge stack (300BB) can pay off lighter but history shows folds (e.g., Hand 104). EV+ vs. calling range weighted to worse straights/pairs.

Calling Elon's turn re-raise with top pair (Qs on Tc-Js-Qd-9h). The pot odds of 2.19:1 justify the call with a marginal hand. Elon appears to be short-stacked and has shown aggressive play throughout, suggesting a wider range than just premium hands. Top pair has decent equity against a range including draws (KJ), weaker pairs (99, 88, etc.), and air. My substantial chip lead allows me to take this spot profitably. The straight-completing turn Nine is concerning, but Elon's all-in-ish aggression is more consistent with desperation than flopped monsters.

Heads-up play on the button with 8d-Tc. This is a reasonable hand in position against Elon who has been quite aggressive throughout. Calling the extra 50 to see the flop is the correct play here because: (1) I have positional advantage as the button, (2) My 26,750 stack provides flexibility to apply pressure post-flop, (3) 8-10 is a connector with decent equity and playability, (4) Acting last post-flop will give me significant information advantage. This is a standard small blind call in heads-up dynamics.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp from Claude. My hand is 9s3h, which is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low card strength, and no suited potential for flush draws. While BB defense range vs SB limp is wide due to getting a free flop and multiway dynamics not applying here (heads-up), 93o is near the bottom of any reasonable range. Raising would be overly spewy with no value or strong bluff equity, and folding costs nothing extra since I'm getting odds to realize any equity. Best play is to check, take a free flop, and play postflop based on board texture and Claude's likely wide limp range from SB. This preserves stack while exploiting position postflop.

Preflop check with 93o was correct to realize equity OOP against a wide SB limp range. Flop Jd7cKd is a dryish high-card board that misses my hand completely—no pair, no draws (guts, flush, etc.). Villain's SB limp range includes many Kx, Jx, QTx, and pairs that connect well here, so my range is heavily uncapped and perceived as weak. This exploits position and range dynamics long-term.

Betting on the flop after Elon checks. Analysis:

1. **Hand evaluation**: 8d-Tc has moderate equity with a gutshot straight draw (any 9 makes a straight) and two unpaired cards. While not a strong made hand, the connectivity provides outs.

2. **Board context**: Jd-7c-Kd is relatively dry with two diamonds. Elon's check suggests he doesn't have a premium holding (no KK, JJ, or KJ).

3. **Positional advantage**: I'm on the button acting last pre-flop and post-flop. This is a significant edge in heads-up play.

4. **Stack dynamics**: Elon has 13,100 remaining (short stack), I have 26,700. His shorter stack makes him vulnerable to aggression.

5. **Betting rationale**: 
   - This is a standard continuation bet in position after he checks
   - 150 chips (75% pot) applies solid pressure without overcommitting
   - Forces Elon to make a decision with a marginal hand
   - If he folds, I win the pot immediately; if he calls, I maintain initiative with two more cards to come

6. **Risk management**: My bet is sizing appropriately given stack sizes and pot odds, maintaining flexibility for turn play while applying maximum pressure on a checking opponent.

Calling with K5o heads-up in position. The 3:1 pot odds justify calling a marginal hand, and I'll have positional advantage post-flop. My stack is healthy relative to Elon's, and with 19 hands remaining, accumulating chips through position play is valuable.

Td4c is a speculative trash hand with minimal high card strength, poor connectivity, and offsuit. In the BB facing a SB limp (Claude's wide range likely includes many playable hands but also junk), raising preflop commits chips unprofitably with low equity and bad playability. Pot odds are favorable (already invested BB, seeing flop cheaply). Plan to check, realize equity if we hit, or play fit-or-fold post-flop. Deep stacks (10.9bb effective? Wait, 50/100 blinds, stacks deep ~100bb+), so no need to spew. Prioritize pot control and position post-flop.

Td4c is complete air on a J-Q-8 rainbow board with straight draw potential (e.g., villain could have 9T, KT, 9x suited connectors). No pair, no flush draw, backdoor nothing meaningful. OOP in BB after checking preflop to SB limp (Claude's wide range: Ax, pairs, suited broadways, connectors). Pot control essential; check to realize equity if villain checks back or pot odds improve, but plan to fold to any reasonable bet as equity <30% vs. villain's continuing range. No bluff raise value OOP deepstacked with reverse implied odds. Fit/fold play maximizes EV long-term.

Elon checked the flop showing weakness. With K-high on a dry broadway board (J♥Q♦8♠), I have a decent hand that beats many weaker holdings. My significant chip lead (28,900 vs 10,900) justifies aggressive play. I'll make a standard continuation bet to take down the pot, as Elon likely whiffed or has a weak hand. This also maintains pressure with 19 hands left.

Td4c is complete air on a J-Q-8 rainbow flop with no pair, no draws, and negligible backdoors. Claude's SB limp preflop is wide, but their flop bet (25% pot) after my check represents a polarized range: value (top pair+, sets, overpairs, combo draws like JQ/98 suited) or bluffs (gutshots like KT/9T, weak Ax). I have ~0% equity against their continuing range and fold equity is minimal for a bluff-raise OOP deepstacked (my 109bb stack risks reverse implied odds and poor playability). Pot odds are ~3:1 (125 to call into 450), but required equity is ~22% which I lack. Best play is to fold cleanly, preserve stack for better spots, and avoid spewing chips in a fit-or-fold dynamic. Long-term EV maximized by tight postflop discipline.

Heads-up pre-flop from SB/Button with Ts5d. This is a speculative hand with straight potential and good position post-flop. Standard opening range from SB heads-up includes T5o (playable ~60% of hands or more). Raising takes initiative, builds the pot, and denies equity to BB calling range. Claude has a deep stack, but with 10k effective, a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate. Limping is suboptimal as it allows BB to check and realize equity; folding is too tight late in a cash game session.

Calling with A3o from the big blind in heads-up play. Elon is short-stacked (10,600) and has been aggressive, suggesting a wide raising range. While A3 is weak, it has an ace that plays well heads-up and improves on many flops. With a 2:1 pot odds and stack advantage, calling to see the flop is the optimal play rather than folding the blind or over-committing by raising.

Elon has checked to me on the flop with 7s 6d Js. I hold Ah3h - just ace high with a gutshot. While I have a strong positional advantage with a larger stack (28.8k vs 10.6k), my current hand strength is quite weak. Elon's check after pre-flop aggression suggests he either missed or has a marginal holding. In heads-up play with limited hand strength, checking is preferable to betting because: (1) I avoid committing chips with just ace high, (2) I get a free card with equity potential (any K gives me a straight, any A improves my hand), and (3) if I improve on the turn, I can apply more confident pressure. If I bet now, I'm either getting called by a better hand or folding out weaker holdings, neither of which maximizes my value. Checking allows me to control the pot size while maintaining my positional advantage on future streets.

Flop board 7s6dJs is draw-heavy with two spades, straight draws like 45, 58, 89, and overcards. I raised preflop with Ts5d from Button, giving me initiative and range advantage including Jx, 87, suited connectors, broadways. Hand has a gutshot straight draw to 8 (5-6-7-8-T), backdoor spade flush draw (Ts with two spades on board), backdoor diamond flush, and Ten overcard. Claude's check is standard from BB calling range, often weak or draws. With position, deep effective stacks (~10k), and late in session, c-bet for thin value, protection, semi-bluff, and fold equity. Half-pot sizing 300 denies equity to his floats/draws and builds pot without overcommitting. Folding/checking misses value from worse.

With Ah3h on a 7s 6d Js flop against short-stacked Elon (10,300 remaining), I should call his 300 bet. Analysis: (1) Hand strength - I have ace high plus a gutshot to the nut straight (any K wins), giving me roughly 20-25% equity with two cards to come; (2) Pot odds - current pot is 900, to call 300 is 3:1, requiring approximately 25% equity to break even, which my hand approximately achieves; (3) Stack dynamics - I have a large chip advantage (28,800 vs 10,300), allowing me to play more hands and control the pace; (4) Elon's action - his check pre-flop followed by this bet suggests a moderate hand or a c-bet that I can continue against; (5) Position - I defend from big blind in heads-up, giving me positional information on subsequent streets if the hand goes to the turn. Calling allows me to see the turn card while maintaining proper pot odds, and if I hit any king or ace, I can apply meaningful pressure given the stack advantage. Folding surrenders reasonable equity; raising risks overcommitting with marginal holdings.

Betting 700 on the turn with a pair of threes and ace kicker. The 3d significantly improved my hand from the flop when I had just ace-high. Against short-stacked Elon (10,300 remaining), applying pressure with a made hand is optimal. This bet size (roughly 58% of pot) is aggressive enough to build value and apply meaningful pressure without overcommitting. Given my large chip advantage (28.5k vs 10.3k), I can afford to play aggressively here. Checking would give Elon a free card and miss value-extraction opportunities. The board (7s 6d Js 3d) doesn't complete obvious draws, making my pair a strong holding.

Facing a turn lead of 700 into 1900 after check-calling my flop c-bet on a draw-heavy board (7s6dJs flop, 3d turn). My Ts5d is T-high with a weak gutshot straight draw (to 4 or 8 for non-straight actually, but marginal equity ~10-15% vs villain's check-call-lead range) and backdoor draws. Villain's line suggests showdown value (Jx, 76, sets/pairs) or strong draws (spade flush draw, 45/89 str8 draws that hit or semi-bluff). Pot odds are ~3.7:1 requiring ~23% equity, but realization is low OOP on river without position advantage here as he leads. Deep stacks but late in session (hand 132/150), folding marginal hand preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity for raise with air, calling commits too much with poor equity. Standard fold to turn lead after cb-shove.

Raising to 200 from the button with pocket threes in heads-up play. With a significant chip advantage over the short-stacked opponent, I apply pressure while maintaining a strong position. Pocket pairs play well in position, and this raise helps build the pot when I'm ahead in the chip stack. Standard opening raise that puts Elon in a decision with limited stack.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small raise to 200 from Claude on the button/small blind. Pot is 300, calling 100 more for great pot odds (3:1). Hand is T4s, a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Against a button range, which is wide and aggressive based on history (Claude raises frequently), T4s has solid equity (~35-40% vs loose button open) and implied odds postflop with position. Folding is too tight in BB defense; raising is unnecessary with a speculative hand. Calling to realize equity and control pot size.

Flop board Jd4s3c is dry with some straight draw potential (e.g., 52 OESD, 56 gutshot) but rainbow. I have middle pair (4s) with T kicker, which blocks some Jx and is ahead of Ax, Kx, Qx bluffs, weaker pairs, and draws in Claude's wide button calling range (based on history of frequent raises/calls). He checked as preflop aggressor, likely weak or trapping, but betting realizes equity, protects against draws, and gets value from worse (e.g., Jx with worse kicker, Ax, overcards). Pot 400, deep stacks (10k effective), bet ~60% pot (250) to build pot while denying equity. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/overcards that can outdraw or bluff later. Folding impossible with top-2 pair-ish strength here.

I have pocket threes and the flop brought me trip threes (3h3s vs Jd 4s 3c board). This is a very strong hand. Elon is short-stacked at 9850 chips and just raised to 250 on the flop. Given my strong holding and his weak chip position, I should raise to build the pot and apply pressure. A raise to 700 (beyond minimum raise of 500) is appropriate to extract value while keeping him engaged - at his stack size, this represents significant pressure. My trips are likely the best hand here, and I want to build the pot for showdown or to force a decision.

Facing a check-raise OOP on a Jd4s3c rainbow board with 4cTc for middle pair top kicker. Preflop, Claude opened wide from button, I defended speculatively. My flop bet targeted his wide checking range (Ax high, draws, weak pairs), but his raise to 700 (~1.75x pot) into 650 polarizes him to strong value (JJ/44/33/J4/J3/53 straight, strong Jx) or semi-bluffs (52/A2/K2 draws). Pot odds 3:1 require ~25% equity, but vs value-heavy check-raise range (~15-20% hands), equity ~18-22% (dominated by better pairs, crushed by sets/straights). Even vs bluffs (possible given aggression), implied odds limited OOP deep. Turn cards like 5/2 complete straight, A/K/Q improve his likely overs, J pairs his trips/set. Raising risks cooler/shove; calling bloats pot (SPR~5 OOP tough). History shows Claude aggressive but capable of value raise; folding middle pair here preserves ~9k stack (98bb) for premium spots in final hands. Tight fold maximizes EV long-term.

Heads-up from SB vs BB, A7o is a strong hand with ace blocker and decent playability. Standard to raise wide range from SB to steal blinds or play postflop in position. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard sizing. Equity realization good if called, and fold equity high given history of Claude folding to raises.

Elon has raised to 300 from the button, and I'm facing a 200 call in the BB with Q6o. This is a weak holding that doesn't warrant continued investment, especially out of position post-flop. While Elon has been playing aggressively throughout the session, Q6 is below the threshold for defending profitably from the BB against his range. The pot odds (2:1) are reasonable but not sufficient for a hand this weak that will play poorly in position. I'm preserving chips for stronger situations where I have better equity. With 16 hands remaining and a healthy stack, folding marginal hands is profitable long-term strategy.

In heads-up play on the button with Js-Td, I should raise with this solid broadway hand. My significant chip advantage (30,000 vs 9,850) allows me to apply consistent pressure. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips gives me positional control post-flop while extracting value from this decent starting hand. Elon has shown a tendency to fold to aggression, making this a profitable play.

Preflop in BB facing a standard button/SB raise to 300 (200 to call into 400 pot, laying ~3:1). Holding A♠3♦, which is offsuit but has an ace blocker against opponent's strong Ax ranges, decent playability for nut flush draws, wheel straights, or ace-high. Vs a wide button opening range (~40-50% typically), equity is around 40-45%, sufficient for the pot odds. Postflop, I act first but can check-fold weak boards or play aggressively with strong Ax hits. Folding too tight in BB defense; 3-bet sizing would be large (~900+), but A3o isn't premium enough for value 3-bet and marginal as bluff given opponent's stack and history of calling stations/light aggression. Claude's history shows frequent preflop raises and postflop continuation betting, but often folds to 3bets or resistance. Prefer calling to realize equity, control pot OOP on favorable boards, and induce bluffs. Effective SPR low (~30), good for postflop play.

Flop Kc Jd Th is a highly coordinated board (K-J-T with broadway potential, Q makes straight, many overpairs, sets, two-pair combos in villain's button raising range). Holding As3d: ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (no gutshot/wheel), weak backdoor diamond flush draw. Blocks villain's AA/AK/AQ/AJ but equity is poor OOP vs c-bet range (~25-35% vs value-heavy bets, better vs bluffs but villain cbets wide per history). SPR ~16 postflop, but donk betting air OOP is -EV (gets called by better, folds out worse). Check-fold maximizes EV: pot control, avoid tough river spots, realize equity only if checked back (rare). Claude aggressive postflop but check allows cheap fold to likely c-bet. Defend wider preflop was correct; folding here disciplined.

With two pair (Jacks and Tens) on a K-J-T board, I have a strong hand that's likely ahead of Elon's range. His check indicates weakness or a marginal hand. Given my massive chip advantage (29.75k vs 9.65k) and his short-stacked situation, I should bet for value. A moderate bet of 250 chips (1/3 of the 600 pot) extracts value from his weaker hands like A-K, K-Q, medium pairs, and 8-9 type hands while giving him odds to call and try to double up. This is better than checking and allowing a free card that could improve his hand or lead to river action where he might make a lucky card.

Flop KcJdTh is extremely draw-heavy and favors villain's Button/SB raising range, which includes broadway combos like KQ, Q9, AJ, ATs, sets (KK/JJ/TT rare), two-pair, and bluffs. A♠3♦ is pure ace-high with no pair, draw, or blocker to nuts beyond AA/AK (which I block somewhat). Equity ~25-35% vs polarized c-bet range OOP, poor for calling 250 into 850 (~3.4:1 needs 23% equity, but post-turn realization low). History shows Claude cbets wide/aggressively postflop but value-bets coordinated boards; folding air avoids bloating pot, tough turn/river spots (e.g., Qx, flush/straight cards). Effective SPR ~7 post-call allows aggression but I'm crushed by calls. Preflop call was fine for multiway potential/defense, but disciplined check-fold here maximizes EV long-term, preserves stack for better spots in final 15 hands.

Heads-up on the button with J4s, a suited connector with good playability and potential for straights or flushes. Position is excellent, allowing control post-flop. Claude in BB defends a wide range. Raising takes initiative, builds pot with position, and has fold equity. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300. My stack of 9600 allows flexibility.

Calling with K8o in heads-up BB versus button raise. Marginal hand but justified by: (1) 3:1 stack advantage allows playing wider range; (2) position post-flop gives me control; (3) heads-up dynamics mean opponent's raising range is wide; (4) K-high has showdown value and multiple ways to improve. Calling allows me to play the hand flexibly post-flop while maintaining aggressive image.

Checking with K8o on As5d7c. My hand completely whiffed the flop with no pair, no draw, and only K-high showdown value. Out of position against aggressive opponent. Checking allows me to gather information, keep pot manageable, and evaluate Elon's actions before committing more chips to a marginal holding. Standard check-and-evaluate strategy on a dry flop with a non-premium hand.

Flop of As5d7c hits my nut flush draw perfectly (10 spade outs left, as board has As and I hold two spades, giving highest possible spade flush). No made hand, but excellent equity (~35-40% vs typical calling range). Rainbow board reduces straight draws. Claude's check after calling preflop raise from BB suggests weakness (missed Ax, weak pairs, bluffs), wide defending range vulnerable to semi-bluff. Deep stacks, position allows me to bet for fold equity, deny equity, or get called by worse. Half-pot bet sizes well: builds pot, good SPR post-call (~10:1). If raised, strong draw justifies call/shove depending on sizing. Checking risks giving free card or facing bet OOP.

Folding K8o on As5d7c when faced with a flop bet. This hand whiffed completely with no pair, no draw, and only King-high. Out of position with limited equity and no improving prospects. With a 3:1 stack advantage, I can afford to fold weak holdings that don't have reasonable equity or draw potential. The marginal value of maintaining aggression doesn't outweigh the fundamental weakness of K8o in this spot.

Calling from the button in heads-up with 96o. My chip advantage and position justify seeing a flop against Elon's BB range. The small additional investment (50 chips) is justified by position and stack depth. I can fold easily on the flop if facing aggression.

Heads-up pre-flop in BB vs SB limp (Claude called 50, pot 200). My hand A♦6♦ is suited ace, strong speculative hand with good playability, position disadvantage but initiative important. Claude's limp suggests wide range, possibly weak or trapping, but history shows he folds often to pre-flop aggression. Raising to build pot OOP with strong hand and take control is better than checking to realize equity cheaply. Standard raise size ~3x BB accounting for dead money. Raise to 300 total (adding 200). Effective stacks deep (98bb for me), pot odds favorable.

Folding 96o to Elon's raise. While I have a significant chip advantage (3:1) and position on the button, 96o is too weak to call a raise with. In heads-up play, even with position advantage, this hand lacks the equity to justify calling 200 more chips. I initially called preflop with hopes of position edge post-flop, but Elon's aggressive raise to 300 forces me to reassess. With 14 hands remaining and a healthy stack advantage, I should be selective and fold marginal hands that lack showdown value. Better spots will come where I can leverage my chip lead more effectively.

Heads-up on the button from the SB with 7c5c, a suited connector with good playability. Already posted SB 50, pot 150, facing BB 100 so to call additional 50 (excellent odds). Folding SB preflop heads-up is suboptimal. Limping is passive and invites BB aggression. Raising is standard to take initiative, build pot with position, and leverage the hand's potential for straights/flushes. Claude has been aggressive but calls reasonably often. Choose a standard size: 2.5x BB to 250. My stack 10k effective, deep enough for postflop play.

Calling with Ad 5h in heads-up big blind. While the hand is weak, the pot odds (2.3:1), short-stack dynamics with Elon at 9,800 chips, and heads-up play requirements make this defensible. I have significant stack advantage and post-flop position, allowing me to leverage my range advantage if I connect.

With Ad5h on a 7s-Qc-Jc board in a heads-up scenario, I have ace-high with limited connectivity to this coordinated board. While the ace provides some showdown value, the board contains broadway cards (Q-J) and two clubs, which could connect with many hands in Elon's likely range. Being out of position with a marginal hand, checking is prudent. This allows me to: (1) control pot size with a weak holding, (2) gather information on Elon's hand strength and aggression, and (3) see another card cheaply. My significant stack advantage (29.7k vs 9.8k) means I can afford to play cautiously. If Elon checks back, I maintain flexibility on the turn. If he bets, I can reassess with better information about his range before committing chips with just ace-high.

Flop: 7s Qc Jc gives me bottom pair (7s paired with 7c), plus nut club flush draw (holding 7c5c with QcJc on board, 4-to-nut flush). Also backdoor straight potential. Very strong hand overall with huge equity (~50%+ vs most ranges). Drewy board with flush and straight draws possible (89o/T9s/KTs). Preflop raiser OOP check from Claude suggests weakness or slowplay, but in position I should c-bet for thin value/protection, charging draws and worse made hands (like Ax, lower pairs). Pot 500, bet 2/3 pot (350) balances range, builds pot while leaving room for turns/rivers. Deep stacks allow multi-street play. Folding or checking would be too passive with this equity.

Calling with Ad5h on 7s-Qc-Jc board in heads-up spot. Stack advantage (3:1) allows me to play marginal hands and gather information. Ace-high has ~30% equity against Elon's continuation bet range. Folding too frequently exploitable in heads-up; calling keeps me active while seeing turn card. Pot odds 350 to win 1200 favorable for my equity range.

The ace on the turn significantly improved my hand from marginal ace-high to top pair (pair of aces with 5 kicker). This is a strong holding on a relatively dry board (7s-Qc-Jc-Ac). The ace was likely a blank for Elon's continuation bet range if he holds non-ace hands like KK, TT, or flush draws, though it could have hit him if he holds AK/AQ/AJ. With my 3:1 stack advantage and first-to-act position on the turn, betting for value is correct. A bet of 600 (roughly half the pot) applies appropriate pressure while extracting value from his marginal holdings and draws. This leverages my superior hand strength and stack position to build the pot and maintain control.

Turn card Ac completes the board with three clubs (Qc, Jc, Ac), giving me the Ac-high flush using Ac-Qc-Jc-7c-5c. This is likely the nuts, as any opponent flush requires exactly two clubs in hand (since board has only three), and better flushes like Kc-Tc are possible but narrow-range outliers. I also have bottom pair for showdown value against non-flush hands. Preflop I raised and c-bet flop, which he called OOP on a draw-heavy 7s-Qc-Jc board. Now he leads turn for 600 into 1800 (pot after ~2400), which could be a weaker flush, Ac-top, two-pair, set, or bluff/value bet. History shows Claude aggressive and calling stations in multi-street pots. With position, massive equity (~90%+ vs non-nut flushes/bluffs), and deep effective stacks (9450), raising for value maximizes EV. Call too passive, risks free river card or small pot; fold absurd. Raise sizing: 3.5x pot-bet (to 2100 total) builds huge pot while allowing room if he shoves. Near end of 150 hands, exploit by extracting from his wide calling range.

Calling 1,500 to win 3,800 with pair of aces (Ad5h) on 7s-Qc-Jc-Ac. Pot odds are 2.53:1, requiring ~28% equity. I estimate 55-60% equity against Elon's turn raise range, which includes AK/AQ/AJ (~40% frequency), but also stronger equity vs QJ and non-ace hands. Stack advantage (3.9:1) and endgame dynamics (hand 138/150) support maintaining aggression. Top pair is likely the best hand; vulnerable but worth protection with positive expected value.

River decision with Ad5h on 7s-Qc-Jc-Ac-4c. I have a pair of aces (top pair), which is a strong holding. The 4c on the river is a complete blank and doesn't significantly improve Elon's likely holdings. 

Elon's turn raise from my 600 bet to 2100 suggests he has a hand (AK, AQ, AJ, KK, or possibly a club draw), but the river 4c doesn't improve most of these except if he held a flush draw that completed. 

Given:
- Elon checked to me on the river (indicating weakness or hand control)
- I have top pair with a clean river
- Pot is 5400, and I need to build value with 12 hands remaining
- Elon has only 7350 behind, suggesting we're nearing bubble/late stage

A value bet of 1200 (roughly 22% of pot) accomplishes several goals: (1) extracts value from weaker ace hands, Queen-high, or complete whiffs, (2) keeps the bet sizing reasonable relative to pot to encourage calls, (3) positions me well for the final hands. This bet gets called by AQ, AJ, or marginal holdings while potentially folding hands like missed draws. With my stack advantage and the endgame situation, winning this pot cleanly or extracting value aligns with my win equity.

River 4c completes four clubs on board (Qc, Jc, Ac, 4c), giving me the nut flush A-Q-J-7-5 clubs with 7c5c. Extremely strong hand, second-to-nuts at worst, losing only to specific higher single-club flushes like KcXc, TcXc, 9cXc, 8cXc (where X doesn't change ranking adversely). Beats all lower flushes (6cXc, 3cXc, 2cXc), no-flush hands, and unlikely boats (no obvious full house combos: board unpaired except flush). Preflop: my button raise, his BB call (loose range). Flop call OOP draw-heavy board. Turn donk 600 into ~1800 then calls my value raise to 2100 (pot-committing-ish), suggesting medium strength/draw hit, not pre-made monster. River small bet 1200 (~1/5 into 6600) after big turn call screams thin value/blocker with medium flush or bluff catcher, unlikely nuts (which would bomb larger or check-raise). Claude's history: calls stations in big pots but folds to aggression sometimes. With position, deep effective (~7k), endgame pressure (138/150), raise extracts max value from worse flushes/no flush; call too passive misses EV; fold insane. Sizing: ~3x his bet (1200*3=3600 total), builds massive pot (~12k+), all-in friendly if he jams (call), balances vs his wide calling range.

Calling with pair of aces on river after Elon's raise. Pot odds of 4.25:1 require only ~19% equity; my pair of aces has ~35%+ equity against Elon's likely range (premium aces, bricked draws, small pairs, and occasional bluff). Positive EV calculation (~240 chips expected value). Endgame dynamics with 12 hands remaining require deploying capital with positive expectation. Stack advantage and Elon's short stack support committing to this marginal positive EV situation. Top pair is likely ahead against most non-premium ace combinations and all missed draws.

Holding 5s8s on the button in heads-up play. I need to call 50 to win a pot of 100, giving me 3:1 odds. While this is a marginal hand, several factors favor a call: (1) I have strong position (button/SB), (2) pot odds justify a call with a suited connector, (3) I'm ahead in stacks (23.6k vs 16.2k) and can afford to play more hands, (4) Elon has been aggressive but I can outplay post-flop with positional advantage, (5) 5-8 suited has reasonable equity (~40-45%) against his button-raising range. This is a disciplined call that exploits position and pot odds rather than folding a playable hand. Post-flop, I'll be able to control the action and fold if I don't hit.

Heads-up preflop in BB facing SB limp from Claude on the button. My hand 5c8h is offsuit, mediocre connectivity, low cards, not suited. Folding BB to limp too tight heads-up. Raising not ideal as it's not strong enough and OOP postflop. Best to check and realize equity on flop cheaply, controlling pot OOP. Deep stacks favor seeing flops with speculative hands.

Flop board 9s 3d Td is somewhat coordinated with possible straight draws (JQ) and a diamond flush draw. My 5c 8h completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot or otherwise), no flush draw. Pure air with zero showdown value heads-up OOP deep-stacked. Claude limped preflop (wide range possible including suited connectors, Ax, pairs). Assuming he checked (action to me with to-call 0), betting is a pure bluff on a board where he can have hits/draws and call/raise light. Poor EV to bluff OOP with nothing; better to check, control pot size, take free card, and fold to aggression while preserving stack. Consistent with preflop plan to realize equity cheaply but fold when we miss.

Checking back the flop with a strong drawing hand (8 straight outs + pair outs) against Elon's checked flop. This keeps the pot small while I have positive equity and can see the turn card. Since Elon showed weakness by checking, getting a free card with 14 outs and top-pair vulnerability is the optimal play. I'm in position to control the action on the turn if I hit my draw.

Heads-up pot on turn with 5c8h, complete air: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw despite backdoor heart with 8h. Board 9s3dTd3h pairs the 3, potentially improving sets (33), two-pairs (93s, T3s), or trips/draws. Claude's SB limp range is wide; checking flop suggests possible weakness, floats, or traps, but doesn't change my nothing. OOP deep-stacked (16k effective), betting is pure bluff into wide calling range on coordinated board where villain hits medium strength often and can raise light. Poor EV to bluff; better to check, take free river card, showdown if checked through, or fold to value/bet without committing stack. Consistent with prior check-fold plan when missing flop.

Elon checked the turn on a 9s-3d-Td-3h board, showing clear weakness. I'm holding 5s8s with a drawing hand (approximately 8-12 outs including wheel possibilities and pair outs). By checking back, I get a free river card to potentially complete my draw while maintaining position. This is the mathematically sound play when my opponent shows weakness and I have a hand that improves significantly on the river. Betting here would risk getting check-raised on marginal equity, whereas checking allows me to see a free card and outplay on the river if I improve or if my opponent bets.

River heads-up OOP with 5c8h complete air: no pair, draw, or showdown value on 9s-3d-Td-3h-6d board. Paired 3s, diamond flush draw completed, possible straights (e.g., 45 for wheel? No, or JQ but unlikely), pairs (9/T/6/3), two pairs (93, T3, etc.). Claude SB limp wide range, checked flop/turn suggesting possible medium strength, floats, or missed draws with showdown value. Pot 200 deep (~80BB effective), first to act: bluff betting risks calls/raises from better hands while thin value unlikely; check preserves stack, induces bluffs/check-behinds from worse air/medium hands, or allows cheap fold to his bet. Optimal check-fold equity realization consistent with prior passive play when missing.

River on a 9s-3d-Td-3h-6d board. I hold 5s8s with a complete whiff - no made hand, just T-high. Elon has checked all streets showing weakness. With 11 hands left and a 7k chip lead, I should avoid unnecessary bluffs. My hand has zero showdown value but his passive line suggests he also has a weak hand. Q4s suited is a strong defensive hand from the BB vs a Button/SB raise in heads-up play. Folding is too tight in positionally advantaged BB defense. 3-betting as a bluff is an option but unnecessary with this hand's post-flop potential; prefer calling to realize equity OOP while controlling pot size preflop. Near end of session (hand 149/150), but no reason to deviate from GTO fundamentals to build pot OOP with speculative suited hand for multiway potential or draws.
